questions.prompt: A key project is behind schedule. What is your first move?
options: Re-scope by impact, align owners, and communicate an updated delivery plan.
Work silently and hope to catch up.
Delay status updates until there is good news.
Add more meetings without changing plan.

questions.prompt: How do you handle competing requests from two stakeholders?
options: Clarify business outcomes, rank by impact and risk, and get explicit priority agreement.
Pick the one who asks most often.
Say yes to both and decide later.
Ignore both until leadership decides.

questions.prompt: Your teammate is underperforming. What do you do?
options: Set clear expectations, offer support, and track progress with regular check-ins.
Complain to others and avoid direct conversation.
Take over their tasks permanently.
Immediately remove them from all projects.

questions.prompt: How do you measure success of a feature launch?
options: Define target metrics before launch and compare outcomes against baseline.
Judge by how hard the team worked.
Assume success if no one complains.
Only track lines of code shipped.

questions.prompt: A production incident occurs late Friday. What is the best response?
options: Stabilize service first, communicate status, then run a blameless postmortem.
Wait until Monday to investigate.
Focus on identifying who caused it.
Disable alerts to reduce noise.

questions.prompt: How do you decide between shipping fast and maintaining quality?
options: Protect critical quality bars, then trade scope not standards.
Always ship fast even if reliability drops.
Never ship unless perfect.
Let engineers decide without product context.

questions.prompt: What is your approach to mentoring junior engineers?
options: Set growth goals, pair on real tasks, and provide specific, frequent feedback.
Give only easy tasks so mistakes are impossible.
Correct every detail without explaining why.
Avoid mentoring to save time.

questions.prompt: How do you communicate technical debt to leadership?
options: Translate debt into delivery risk, incident risk, and cost with concrete examples.
Use only deep technical terms.
Hide debt because it sounds negative.
Request a full rewrite immediately.

questions.prompt: A project failed to meet goals. What does strong ownership look like?
options: Share accountability, analyze root causes, and commit to corrective actions.
Deflect blame to requirements.
Declare the goals unrealistic and move on.
Avoid discussing failure publicly.

questions.prompt: How would you build trust with a new cross-functional partner?
options: Align on expectations early, follow through consistently, and surface risks proactively.
Promise aggressive timelines before technical review.
Keep communication minimal to reduce interruptions.
Only engage when blockers appear.
